title: Sub-50 Picosecond exceptionally Bright Perovskite Scintillation by Unlocking Giant Oscillator Strength
authors: Chuanwei Dai, Yunbiao Zhao, Xiao Ouyang, Huaqing Huang, Yulan Liang, Jiaqi Bai, Yingjie Song, Jianhan Sun, Yiqun Duan, Wenjun Ma, Senlin Huang, Shufeng Wang, Jianming Xue, Xiaoping Ouyang
abstract: Ultrafast scintillators are indispensable for precise timing in high-energy physics and medical diagnostics. Fundamentally constrained by the trade-off between emission rate and light yield, conventional scintillators remain kinetically trapped in the sub-nanosecond regime, failing to break 50-picosecond limit. Here, we demonstrate a strategy to bypass this limitation by harnessing the coherent radiative acceleration in weakly confined CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals to generate an ultrafast photon burst. This effect originates from the giant oscillator strength, which we unlock by suppressing exciton-phonon scattering at mild cryogenic temperatures. Consequently, our scintillator achieves an unprecedented dominant lifetime of 13.11 ps alongside a high light yield of 21,851 ph/MeV. The resulting prompt photon emission rate more than 100 times higher than that of state-of-the-art ultrafast scintillators. We validate this breakthrough in realistic detection scenarios, achieving a coincidence time resolution of 30.8 ps and accurately resolving 13.5 ps electron bunches and 16.6 ps single-shot gamma-ray pulses. Our findings establish a robust coherent framework for next-generation ultrafast scintillators, pushing extreme radiation diagnostics into the picosecond frontier.
summary: This paper presents a breakthrough in scintillator technology using CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals. By suppressing exciton-phonon scattering at cryogenic temperatures, the researchers unlocked a giant oscillator strength that enables ultra-fast photon bursts. This allows for a scintillator lifetime of just 13.11 ps with a very high light yield. The results enable unprecedented timing resolution for high-energy physics and medical imaging applications.
main_problem: Conventional scintillators are limited by a trade-off between emission rate and light yield, preventing them from breaking the 50-picosecond timing limit.
main_result: The researchers achieved a sub-50 ps scintillator with a 13.11 ps dominant lifetime and high light yield (21,851 ph/MeV) by unlocking giant oscillator strength in perovskite nanocrystals.
method: The study uses weakly confined CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals at mild cryogenic temperatures to suppress exciton-phonon scattering and harness coherent radiative acceleration.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of weakly confined CsPbCl3 perovskite nanocrystals operating at mild cryogenic temperatures.
key_observables: Dominant lifetime, light yield (ph/MeV), prompt photon emission rate, coincidence time resolution (CTR), and resolution of electron/gamma-ray pulses.
important_parameters: 13.11 ps lifetime, 21,851 ph/MeV light yield, 30.8 ps coincidence time resolution, and 13.5-16.6 ps pulse resolution.
main_assumptions: The effect relies on suppressing exciton-phonon scattering via mild cryogenic temperatures.
paper_structure: Introduction to scintillator limitations, demonstration of the strategy using CsPbCl3 nanocrystals, characterization of lifetime and light yield, and validation in realistic detection scenarios.
why_interesting: The paper demonstrates the manipulation of giant oscillator strength and coherent radiative acceleration in nanocrystals, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and the control of light-matter interactions.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
